meta_description: Three Speexify members on the moment their English stopped being the problem.
index_sub: Three members. Three moments when the language stopped being the thing in the way.
index_cta_title: Your story is one decision away.
index_cta_sub: Book a first session. The practice ground is open.
story1_shift: From asking someone else to take the call — to opening it.
story1_before: Sorry, can someone else take this? My English isn't…
story1_after: I'll kick us off. Here's where we are and what we need.
story1_pull_quote: I didn't get better at English. I stopped getting in my own way.
story1_para1: Sara had been at the company two years before she ran her first client call. Not because she wasn't ready — she'd been ready for a year. She just kept finding reasons it should be someone else.
story1_para2: "The English was the excuse," she says now. "It was always the excuse." Sara's English, on paper, was fine. She'd studied for fifteen years. She read in English every day. The problem wasn't the language. It was the half-second between thinking and speaking — the gap where she'd already decided it would come out wrong.
story1_para3: The first session with her coach didn't touch grammar. They opened a doc and listed the calls she'd ducked that month. Then the ones she'd actually run, and what made those ones feel safe. By the third session, the doc became a script for the kind of call she kept avoiding.
story1_para4: She ran her first 12-person client kickoff in week eleven. "Halfway through I realised I'd disagreed with the client. In English. Out loud. And then I'd proposed a different timeline. I didn't notice myself doing it. That was the part that shocked me."
story1_para5: She still uses the doc. It's mostly empty now.
story1_outcome: Sara now leads weekly client kickoffs and runs the customer review with her director. She still doesn't think her English is perfect. She's stopped checking.
story2_shift: From muting himself on standups — to running the architecture review.
story2_before: Umm… so what I mean is… maybe we could… sorry.
story2_after: Here's what I think. We ship this and test with users next week.
story2_pull_quote: I stopped translating in my head about six weeks in. Now I just say what I mean.
story2_para1: Ahmed had been writing production code in English for nine years. He'd never said a full sentence to the CTO. When the London office did standups, he muted his mic and typed in the chat.
story2_para2: "My engineers reported to me in Arabic," he says, "and then I'd translate everything for the leadership update. I was doing two jobs. Badly."
story2_para3: The work with his coach started with one thing: the standup. Sixty seconds, every weekday. They drafted a structure together — three lines, in order, every time. Same structure for weeks. Then they began breaking it. A joke. An opinion. A pushback when something didn't make sense.
story2_para4: By the third month his coach asked him to run a thirty-minute architecture review with the visiting CTO. "I prepared for two days. Halfway through, I was at the whiteboard explaining a trade-off, and I noticed I wasn't searching for the next word. I was just talking. That had never happened before."
story2_para5: His standups are no longer scripted. Sometimes they're a joke. He hasn't muted his mic since February.
story2_outcome: Ahmed now leads architecture reviews in English and runs the weekly sync with the London office. His next interview will be for a staff engineer role at a US fintech. He's already booked it.
story3_shift: From asking a colleague to translate — to drafting the proposal herself.
story3_before: Can you look at this before I send it? I don't trust my tone.
story3_after: Sent the draft to the CEO. Reply: 'Your tone was perfect.'
story3_pull_quote: My English stopped sounding like English. It started sounding like me.
story3_para1: Yara had been writing internal emails in Arabic for a decade. When the company opened a UAE office and her remit became regional, she had a problem: every external email had to go through a colleague who 'sounded more native.'
story3_para2: "I was technically the one writing them," she says. "But I'd write a draft, send it to her, she'd rewrite it, I'd send it. It made me feel like a junior. I am not a junior."
story3_para3: Her coach started with one document: a real proposal Yara had to send the following week. They didn't fix it. They rebuilt it. Out loud, line by line. Yara read it. Her coach asked: would you say that, in a meeting, to a person? If not — what would you actually say?
story3_para4: After two months, Yara stopped sending things to her colleague to rewrite. After four, her CEO replied to one of her proposals with three words: 'Your tone was perfect.'
story3_para5: "I read it twice. He'd never said that. About anything I'd written. In any language."
story3_outcome: Yara now leads the Gulf expansion's external communications. She drafts every proposal herself. The colleague who used to rewrite her emails reports to her now.
detail_cta_title: Want a chapter of your own?
detail_cta_sub: The first session is on us. No course. No commitment. Just a real conversation.
